Transcript:
Financial results for Costco Wholesale
For the first quarter of fiscal 2024, Costco Wholesale reported a strong financial performance. Net sales reached $56.72 billion, up 6.1% from $53.44 billion in the same quarter of the previous year. This growth benefited slightly (about 1.0-1.5%) from a favorable shift in the fiscal calendar due to a 53rd week in the prior fiscal year. Membership fee income climbed to $1.082 billion, an increase of 8.2% year-over-year, reflecting continued growth in paid household members (now at 72.0 million, up 7.6% year-over-year) and high renewal rates of 92.8% in the U.S./Canada and 90.5% worldwide. Executive members represented over 46% of paid memberships and accounted for 73% of sales, with executive member numbers rising by 939,000 during the quarter.
Net income for the quarter was $1.509 billion ($3.58 per share), a 16.5% increase from $1.364 billion ($3.07 per share) in the prior year’s first quarter. This year’s results included a $44 million tax benefit from stock-based compensation, whereas last year saw a $53 million tax benefit for stock-based compensation and a $93 million pre-tax charge largely related to downsizing charter shipping operations. Gross margin improved by 43 basis points to 11.04% (up from 10.61%), and even after factoring out gasoline price deflation, gross margin rose by 36 basis points. However, selling, general, and administrative (SG&A) expenses as a percentage of sales increased by 25 basis points to 9.45% compared to 9.20% in the previous year, mainly due to wage increases and the costs associated with opening more warehouses.
Operating income inferred from the higher net income and improved gross margin also showed positive growth, despite the increased SG&A ratio. Interest expense for the quarter rose slightly to $38 million (up $4 million year-over-year), while interest income and other income surged by $107 million to $160 million, driven by higher interest rates and larger cash balances. Costco’s income tax rate for the quarter was 24.5%, up from 23.0% a year earlier.
E-commerce sales, after several quarters of decline, returned to growth with a 6.1% year-over-year increase (excluding foreign exchange impacts). This growth was especially strong in categories such as appliances (mid-20% growth), tires (low teens), and food gift cards (mid-teens). Costco also declared a special cash dividend of $15 per share (worth about $6.7 billion in total), payable in January, marking its fifth such dividend in the past 11 years and demonstrating its strong capital position, as no new debt was required to support this payout.
Capital expenditures for the quarter were around $1.04 billion, with projected full-year capex in the $4.4–$4.6 billion range, up slightly from $4.3 billion in the prior fiscal year. During the first quarter, Costco opened 10 new warehouses (one relocation), resulting in a net gain of nine locations (eight in the U.S., one in Canada). The company plans to open 33 new locations (net 31) during fiscal 2024, up from 23 net openings in fiscal 2023.
Costco’s bottom-line profit grew notably, with reported net income up 16.5% over the prior year’s quarter. Strong drivers included revenue growth from both merchandise sales and membership income, improved gross margin, and a rebound in e-commerce sales. While operational expenses increased due to labor and expansion efforts, these were outweighed by higher sales and continued operational efficiencies. Robust membership engagement and renewal rates further bolstered recurring revenue streams and profitability. Costco’s ongoing expansion and capital investments, coupled with healthy core business growth and a disciplined financial approach, allowed the company to serve members effectively while also returning significant capital to shareholders.

Financial results for Adobe
In fiscal year 2023, Adobe generated total revenue of $19.41 billion, representing 13% annual growth, and achieved record profitability with GAAP earnings per share (EPS) of $11.82 and non-GAAP EPS of $16.07, both up 17% year-over-year. Adobe’s operating cash flow was $7.3 billion for the year. The company ended the year with remaining performance obligations (RPO) of $17.22 billion, up 13% from the previous year, highlighting strong forward revenue visibility. Adobe also returned value to shareholders through the repurchase of 11.5 million shares at a cost of $4.63 billion.
Adobe’s key business segment, Digital Media, which includes Creative Cloud and Document Cloud, produced $14.22 billion in revenue during 2023, while the Digital Experience segment delivered $4.89 billion in revenue. In the fourth quarter of 2023, Adobe achieved a new quarterly revenue record of $5.05 billion, growing 13% year-over-year. Profitability accelerated in Q4, with GAAP EPS at $3.23 (28% growth) and non-GAAP EPS at $4.27 (19% growth). Operating cash flow in Q4 was $1.6 billion, and Adobe repurchased 1.8 million shares in the quarter.
Within the Digital Media segment for Q4, revenue reached $3.72 billion (13–14% growth), with a record net new digital media annualized recurring revenue (ARR) of $569 million, taking the ARR at year-end to $15.17 billion. Creative Cloud contributed $3.0 billion in revenue in Q4, up 14%, with $398 million in net new ARR, driven by strong individual and commercial demand, Adobe Stock’s best-ever performance, and rising adoption of Firefly and Express. Document Cloud contributed Q4 revenue of $721 million, up 17%, with a record $171 million net new ARR, propelled by strong subscription growth, transition from free to paid users, mobile momentum, and strong enterprise sales.
The Digital Experience business reported Q4 revenue of $1.27 billion (10–11% growth), with subscription revenue at $1.12 billion, up 12%. This segment’s growth has been supported by strong bookings, large multi-solution deals, Experience Platform adoption, solid retention, and an emerging pipeline for GenStudio. 
Throughout 2023, Adobe’s earnings per share outpaced revenue growth, reflecting strong margins and disciplined investment, particularly in R&D, marketing, and sales. Management emphasizes continued world-class margin performance.
Looking ahead to fiscal year 2024, Adobe’s guidance calls for revenue between $21.3 and $21.5 billion, with Digital Media revenue targeted at $15.75 to $15.85 billion and Digital Experience revenue expected between $5.275 and $5.375 billion. Guidance for net new Digital Media ARR is approximately $1.9 billion. GAAP EPS is projected between $13.45 and $13.85, and non-GAAP EPS between $17.60 and $18.00. For Q1 2024, Adobe expects revenue of $5.1 to $5.15 billion, Digital Media revenue of $3.77 to $3.80 billion, Digital Experience revenue of $1.27 to $1.29 billion, GAAP EPS of $3.35 to $3.40, and non-GAAP EPS of $4.35 to $4.40.
As of the end of Q4, Adobe held $7.84 billion in cash and short-term investments, and had $2.15 billion remaining in its share repurchase program. The tax rate for 2023 stood at about 18% on a GAAP basis and 18.5% on a non-GAAP basis.
In summary, Adobe delivered strong top-line and bottom-line growth in 2023, reaching new highs for both revenue and profitability, while generating robust cash flow and executing on substantial share repurchases. All business segments performed well, and management projects continued healthy revenue and profit expansion into fiscal 2024.

Financial results for Oracle
Oracle delivered strong financial performance in the recent quarter, fueled primarily by rapid growth in its cloud services and recurring revenue streams. Total revenues for the quarter reached $12.9 billion, marking a 4% increase year-over-year when including Cerner, and a 6% increase excluding Cerner. The company’s cloud services and license support segment generated $9.6 billion, which now constitutes 74% of total revenue—this figure rose by 11% year-over-year, underscoring a continued shift toward high-quality, recurring revenue sources.
Breaking down the cloud business, application subscription revenues (including support) were $4.5 billion, up 9% from the prior year. Infrastructure subscription revenues (including license support) came in at $5.2 billion, growing by 12%. Total cloud revenue (combining SaaS and IaaS) was $4.8 billion including Cerner (up 24%) and $4.1 billion excluding Cerner (up 25%). Notably, infrastructure-as-a-service (IaaS) revenues surged 50% year-over-year to $1.6 billion, while SaaS revenues increased 14% to $3.2 billion. Gen2 Infrastructure Cloud Services saw a particularly strong annualized growth rate of 55%, with $6 billion in annualized revenue, and Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) consumption revenue exploded by 71%. Strategic back-office SaaS applications reported an annualized revenue of $7.1 billion, climbing 19% year-over-year.
Among other key revenue streams, database subscription revenues (including license support) rose by 4%, with Exadata Database Cloud Services and Autonomous Database growing 40% and 26%, respectively. Traditional software license revenues were $1.2 billion, down 19% due to tough comparisons against the previous year’s elevated results. The recurring portion of Oracle's revenue mix expanded to 74%, while non-recurring revenues slipped to 26%, highlighting Oracle’s ongoing transition away from less predictable, legacy business models.
From a profitability standpoint, Oracle reported earnings per share (EPS) at the high end of its guidance, though no specific profit or margin figures were disclosed. The drivers behind this solid profit performance are attributed to impressive growth across Oracle Cloud Infrastructure, strategic back-office SaaS, and expanding database services. Customers are increasingly choosing Oracle’s cloud platforms for their price-performance benefits and enhanced multi-cloud capabilities.
In summary, Oracle is demonstrating accelerating momentum in the high-value, rapidly growing cloud market, underpinned by robust increases in recurring revenue streams, strong customer demand—especially for AI-ready infrastructure—, and performance at or above guidance on profitability metrics. The company’s legacy, non-recurring businesses now represent a smaller share of total revenue, supporting Oracle’s goal of consistent, high-quality growth. Excluding the impact of the Cerner acquisition, revenue growth figures would be even more pronounced, reinforcing the strength of the core business transformation toward cloud and subscription-based offerings.

Financial results for Salesforce:
Salesforce reported strong financial results in its most recent quarter, achieving revenue of $8.7 billion, which reflects an 11% year-over-year increase (10% in constant currency). Profitability improved markedly, with a non-GAAP operating margin of 31.2% for the quarter—up 850 basis points compared to the prior year—and a year-over-year free cash flow increase of 1,088% to $1.4 billion, alongside a 389% surge in operating cash flow to $1.5 billion. These metrics were supported by disciplined expense management, a reduction in GAAP sales and marketing costs as a percentage of revenue by 6 percentage points, and a renewed focus on the company’s core business growth.
Geographically, Salesforce posted robust revenue growth across all major regions in Q3: revenues in the Americas rose 9%, EMEA saw 14% growth (10% in constant currency), and the APAC region grew by 18% (21% in constant currency), with standout new business traction in India, Brazil, and Japan. On the back of strong deal momentum, Salesforce’s Remaining Performance Obligation (RPO) increased 21% year-over-year to $48.3 billion, and current RPO (CRPO) rose 14% to $23.9 billion.
Profitability continued to expand as Salesforce raised its full-year FY24 non-GAAP operating margin guidance to 30.5%—an 800 basis point improvement over the prior year. Account Executive productivity increased by more than 30% year-over-year, which further supported the company’s margin improvement and operating leverage.
Salesforce also capitalized on multi-cloud opportunities, as deals over $1 million grew 80% year-over-year. The company highlighted that nine out of the top ten deals included six or more Salesforce cloud solutions. Data Cloud was a major growth engine, landing over 1,000 new customers during the quarter and tripling average annual recurring revenue per win compared to the previous quarter. Add-on product revenues—such as for performance management, digital service, and sales productivity—increased nearly 40% year-over-year.
Product-wise, Data Cloud usage accelerated with 6.4 trillion records ingested in Q3 (up 140%) and 1.4 trillion activations (up 220%). MuleSoft was a leading contributor, supporting eight out of the top ten deals and processing 140 billion automated flows, an increase of 142% year-over-year. Slack, integrated into multiple large deals, and Tableau, fully embedded in Data Cloud, also drove incremental value. Einstein GPT Copilots saw adoption across 17% of Fortune 100 companies, confirming uptake of Salesforce’s AI offerings.
Strong financial performance translated into significant capital returns, with $1.9 billion returned to shareholders in Q3 buybacks and more than $10 billion repurchased in the past five quarters. The company expects a net decrease in share count for the first time, effectively counterbalancing stock-based compensation.
For the full fiscal year 2024, Salesforce anticipates revenue between $34.75 billion and $34.8 billion, equating to 11% year-over-year growth, while absorbing a $50 million FX headwind. The company maintains a non-GAAP operating margin target of 30.5%, and forecasts GAAP diluted EPS in the range of $3.99 to $4.00 (including restructuring charges), with non-GAAP diluted EPS expected at $8.18 to $8.19. Free cash flow is projected to rise by 33% to 36%, with FY24 capital expenditures set at slightly below 2.5% of revenue. For Q4, Salesforce expects revenue between $9.18 billion and $9.23 billion, also reflecting a 10% year-over-year increase.
In summary, Salesforce delivered robust double-digit revenue growth, record profit margins, and a substantial increase in free cash flow in the most recent quarter and for its FY24 outlook. The company demonstrated strong execution on large, multi-cloud transactions, realized notable gains in productivity and operational discipline, and returned significant capital to shareholders. Looking ahead, Salesforce is positioned to finish the fiscal year with its highest-ever margins, strong cash generation, and ongoing growth momentum driven by expanding product adoption and enhanced efficiency.

Financial results for NetApp
NetApp’s financial results for Q2 FY’24 demonstrated robust profitability even as total revenue declined on a year-over-year basis. Revenue for the quarter was $1.6 billion, a 6% decrease year-over-year (or 8% lower when accounting for currency effects), yet still above the midpoint of company guidance. Q2 billings were $1.5 billion, representing a 9% year-over-year decrease, and 11% lower adjusted for foreign exchange.
Within revenue segments, NetApp’s Hybrid Cloud revenue reached $1.4 billion (down 7% year-over-year), with product revenue at $706 million (down 16%) and support revenue at $623 million (up 3%). The Public Cloud segment showed growth, delivering $154 million (up 8% year-over-year, flat quarter-over-quarter); Public Cloud’s gains are largely attributable to strong first-party and marketplace cloud services through hyperscalers, which grew over 30% year-over-year despite continued declines in subscription cloud services. The all-flash array product line demonstrated particular strength, with an annualized revenue run rate of $3.2 billion, up 14% quarter-over-quarter. Quarter-over-quarter, Hybrid Cloud segment revenue grew 10%, reflecting sequential improvement.
Profitability metrics reached all-time highs, with Q2 consolidated gross margin of 72%, up 580 basis points from the prior year, driven by favorable product mix, lower component costs—particularly for NAND—strategic purchasing, and continued price discipline. Product gross margin also hit a record at 61% (up 1,080 basis points year-over-year). Operating margin improved to 27% (up 320 basis points year-over-year, including an 80 basis point benefit from FX), and is expected at approximately 26% for the full fiscal year, with Q3 guidance at 28%. Earnings per share (EPS) reached $1.58 for the quarter, up 7% year-over-year, with a $0.07 currency tailwind; full-year EPS is guided to $6.05 to $6.25, with Q3 expected at $1.64 to $1.74.
Cash generation remained strong. Operating cash flow for Q2 was $135 million (temporarily impacted by seasonal tax payments and lower collections), while year-to-date operating cash flow increased 19% to $588 million. Free cash flow stood at $97 million for Q2 and $515 million year-to-date, up 46% compared to the previous period. NetApp returned $403 million to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases in Q2, reducing diluted share count by 4% compared to Q2 last year. Quarter-end cash and short-term investments totaled $2.6 billion, with net cash of approximately $230 million. Deferred revenue registered just below $4 billion, slightly down year-over-year.
Looking ahead, NetApp updated its FY’24 guidance to reflect improved profitability expectations. Annual revenue is now projected to decline around 2% year-over-year, a more optimistic outlook than previous guidance. The company anticipates consolidated gross margin at about 71%, driven in part by continued mix shift toward higher-margin all-flash products. Product gross margin is expected in the 58–60% range. Q3 revenue guidance is set between $1.51 billion and $1.67 billion, with an implied midpoint year-over-year growth of 4%. Operating margin is expected to be about 28% in Q3, sustaining elevated profitability. The company also plans to return at least 100% of free cash flow to shareholders through dividends and buybacks in FY’24, and expects operating cash flow for the year to align closely with net income, assuming typical working capital timing.
In summary, NetApp overcame challenging macro conditions and soft IT spending by focusing on operational discipline, higher-margin segments, and cost management. These efforts led to record profitability—even as topline revenue declined—and robust returns to shareholders. The company’s strategic emphasis on all-flash and hyperscaler cloud services, coupled with effective component sourcing, have positioned it well for sustained strong gross and operating margins, solid free cash flow generation, and continued capital returns to investors throughout the remainder of FY’24.

Financial results for Walmart
Walmart reported robust financial results for the third quarter, highlighted by significant revenue and profit growth across both its core retail operations and digitally enabled business lines. Net sales increased by 4.4% in constant currency, adding nearly $7 billion compared to the same period last year. Walmart U.S. saw a 4.9% increase in comparable sales, while Sam’s Club U.S. posted a 3.8% rise, excluding fuel. Walmart International revenues rose by 5.4% in constant currency, with standout performances from Walmex, which exceeded 9% growth, and China, which was up 25%. E-commerce remained a major growth engine: Walmart U.S. e-commerce sales grew by 24%, Sam’s Club e-commerce sales by 16%, and global e-commerce sales increased 15%. Notably, India-focused PhonePe reached an annualized total payment volume of $1.2 trillion, with impressive user and transaction metrics.
On the profit side, Walmart expanded its gross margin by 32 basis points year-over-year, partly due to lower markdowns and improved supply chain management, although some margin pressures from sales mix persisted. Walmart U.S. gross margin grew modestly by 5 basis points. Adjusted operating income was up 3% in the quarter, aided by favorable currency impacts, although growth was a bit below internal targets due to higher SG&A expenses—especially legal accruals and some wage-related costs. Adjusted earnings per share (EPS) for the quarter was $1.53, a 2% year-over-year increase and above the company’s guidance, with Sam’s Club contributing positively via the LIFO reserve release. Return on investment (ROI) improved by 130 basis points to 14.1% over the past year, lifted by productivity initiatives and favorable comparisons to last year’s legal settlements.
Looking ahead, Walmart raised its full-year sales guidance to growth of 5% to 5.5% and expects operating income to grow 7% to 7.5%, though results are anticipated closer to the lower end of that range due to about 60 basis points in additional costs, mainly from legal expenses and storm recovery in Mexico. Full-year EPS guidance was revised upward to $6.40 to $6.48, reflecting improved operating results and lower interest expenses.
Several key factors influenced Walmart’s profitability and revenue during the quarter. While the company managed expenses and inventories tightly—companywide inventory was down 1.2%, with a 5% reduction in Walmart U.S.—higher-than-expected SG&A expenses from legal costs and store remodels were a headwind. Walmart also benefited from omnichannel growth, higher-margin services such as advertising (global ad revenue up ~20% in Q3), and growth in membership income (Sam’s Club membership income up over 7%). Continued investments in automation, digital fulfillment, and supply chain efficiencies reduced delivery costs and are expected to support future margin expansion. Nonetheless, the company remains cautious about potential variability in consumer demand, food price normalization, and overall economic conditions possibly limiting Q4 sales momentum.
In summary, Walmart delivered strong top-line growth and improved profit margins, outperformed its own sales and EPS expectations for the quarter, and increased its full-year revenue and EPS guidance. Solid e-commerce gains, productivity improvements, and tight capital management contributed to these results, helping offset headwinds from legal and other incremental costs. Walmart remains confident in its business momentum but acknowledges macroeconomic uncertainties may impact consumer behavior in coming periods.

Financial results for The Walt Disney Company:
For fiscal year 2023, The Walt Disney Company saw total revenues increase by 7% year-over-year and segment operating income grow by 8%, driven predominantly by improved performance across the Entertainment, Experiences, and Sports segments. Free cash flow almost reached $5 billion, which marked a substantial increase from the previous period. Diluted earnings per share, excluding certain items, were reported at $0.82 for the fourth quarter and $3.76 for the full year. Notably, adjusted earnings per share for the fiscal fourth quarter nearly tripled compared to the prior year, reflecting marked improvements across all core business segments.
The company achieved major advancements in its cost efficiency and restructuring efforts, being on track for approximately $7.5 billion in cost reductions—over $2 billion above its initial target. This included the elimination of more than 8,000 roles and increased SG&A and operating expense efficiency targets, as well as a planned reduction in entertainment cash content spend by $4.5 billion annually (excluding sports rights and strike impacts) by fiscal 2024.
Segment breakdown for Q4 2023:
In the Entertainment segment, operating income rose by more than $800 million compared to the year-ago quarter. Within direct-to-consumer streaming (Disney+, Hulu, excluding ESPN+), operating losses narrowed significantly by nearly $1 billion year-over-year, with Q4 losses at $420 million, down from a $1.4 billion loss in the prior year. The combined streaming businesses, including ESPN+, showed a Q4 operating loss of $387 million—a year-over-year improvement of over $1 billion and a sequential improvement of $125 million. Disney+ core subscribers surpassed 112 million at fiscal year-end, with close to 7 million added in the fourth quarter, and the ad-supported tier reached 5.2 million subscribers, having added 2 million in Q4. Disney+ average revenue per user increased sequentially thanks to favorable pricing and advertising efforts, although Hulu’s ad revenue slightly declined.
The Sports segment, which includes ESPN and related businesses, posted a 14% increase in operating income versus the prior year. The domestic ESPN business experienced revenue and operating income growth in both 2022 and 2023. While ESPN linear advertising revenue saw a 1% rise, affiliate revenue decreased by less than 5%, attributed mainly to a subscriber decline and the impact of the Charter blackout, partially offset by higher rates. Streaming losses in sports (ESPN+) narrowed and are expected to continue improving into fiscal 2024.
In the Experiences segment, which covers theme parks, cruise lines, and vacation clubs, Q4 operating results rose more than 30% year-over-year, and were up 27% compared to fiscal 2019. Operating margins improved to 22% in Q4, a rise of three percentage points from the prior year. International operations, Disney Cruise Line, Disney Vacation Club, and Disneyland Resort demonstrated strong growth, while Walt Disney World was impacted by accelerated depreciation and inflationary pressures. Domestic parks reported that return on invested capital nearly doubled over five years, and operating margins in Experiences expanded by nearly 300 basis points over the same timeframe.
Looking ahead, Disney projects that its combined streaming businesses (Disney+, Hulu, ESPN+) will achieve profitability in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2024. The Experiences segment is expected to maintain robust annual operating income growth, particularly in international parks and Disney Cruise Line. Free cash flow for fiscal 2024 is anticipated to be about $8 billion, approaching pre-pandemic levels. Capital expenditures are forecast to increase to $6 billion, focusing on investment in parks and cruises, while annual content spend is set to decrease to $25 billion, with sports rights comprising more than 40% of the total. Strong free cash flow is expected to support renewed dividend payments and potential share buybacks.
Strategic initiatives with direct impacts on revenue and profit include the integration of Disney+ and Hulu into a unified app experience, further expansion of ad-supported streaming (with over half of new U.S. Disney+ subscribers in Q4 choosing the ad tier), continued cost discipline, reduced studio content output in favor of higher-quality core franchises, and significant investments in experiential businesses designed for long-term growth and improved returns.
In summary, The Walt Disney Company’s fiscal 2023 saw substantial gains in revenue, operating income, and free cash flow, propelled by cost-saving measures, improvements in the streaming segment, and outstanding performance in Experiences. The anticipated return to streaming profitability and continued growth in Experiences, together with ongoing efficiency, are expected to further strengthen Disney’s financial performance in fiscal 2024.

Financial results for eBay: In Q3 2023, eBay reported revenue of $2.5 billion, reflecting a 4% year-over-year increase on an organic FX-neutral basis, with total revenue growth at 5% after incorporating M&A. Gross Merchandise Volume (GMV) stood at $18 billion, roughly flat on a total FX-neutral basis and down 1% organically compared to the prior year. The company achieved strong growth in advertising revenue, which reached $366 million—a 24% yearly increase—with first-party ads contributing $345 million, now just over 2% of GMV. The take rate improved to 13.9%, up 50 basis points year-over-year, primarily due to advertising growth.
Profit-wise, eBay posted a non-GAAP operating margin of 26.4%, down around 2.5 percentage points from the previous year, impacted by recent M&A, increased product development costs, the expansion of international shipping, FX headwinds, and legal accruals. Non-GAAP earnings per share rose 3% to $1.03, while GAAP EPS was $2.46, bolstered by unrealized gains on equity investments, notably Adevinta. Free cash flow was strong at $777 million, a 23% increase year-over-year, and the company returned $783 million to shareholders via $650 million in share repurchases and $132 million in dividends. Since early 2022, eBay has returned approximately $5.2 billion to shareholders, exceeding its cumulative free cash flow by 25%.
On the operational front, eBay’s focus categories—such as motors parts & accessories, refurbished goods, and luxury items—continued to outperform, growing roughly seven points faster than the rest of the marketplace. Collectibles GMV reached $10 billion over the trailing twelve months, with new features like the Vault and card grading driving high buyer retention and activity. While U.S. GMV declined 2% organically and international GMV was flat on an FX-neutral basis, international shipping improvements and advertising product adoption (over 2.3 million sellers in Q3) helped offset weakness, particularly in challenging European markets.
In terms of cost and capital management, eBay is increasing investment in product development while realizing efficiencies in sales and marketing. General and administrative expenses included a $50 million legal accrual. Capital expenditures are expected at 4-5% of revenue for the year, while the non-GAAP tax rate is 16.5%. The balance sheet remains solid, with $5.4 billion in cash and investments against $7.7 billion in gross debt, and an equity portfolio valued at $4.5 billion at quarter-end.
Looking ahead, Q4 guidance calls for GMV between $17.9 billion and $18.3 billion, an organic FX-neutral decline of 4% to 2% year-over-year, and revenue between $2.47 billion and $2.53 billion, an organic FX-neutral change of -1% to +2%. Non-GAAP operating margin is expected in the 26.1%–26.7% range, with Q4 non-GAAP EPS projected at $1.00–$1.05, which could mean a 2% to 6% year-over-year decrease. Free cash flow for the full year is expected just under $2 billion, although Q4 free cash flow will be minimal due to the timing of cash tax payments. The company reiterates its commitment to return at least 125% of free cash flow to shareholders via buybacks and dividends through 2024.
In summary, eBay’s Q3 2023 performance demonstrated steady revenue growth and resilient profitability despite macroeconomic headwinds, especially in consumer discretionary categories and European markets. The company continues to enhance its profitability through disciplined cost management, innovation in advertising and focus categories, and robust capital returns while adopting a cautious outlook for the holiday quarter and 2024.

Financial results for Target
Target’s 2023 financial performance reveals stable, high revenue levels but notable short-term revenue pressures offset by significant profit improvements. The company’s annual revenue remains well over $100 billion, though in Q3 2023 total revenue declined by 4.2%, primarily due to a 4.3% drop in sales and a 0.6% reduction in other revenue streams. Comparable sales for the quarter fell 4.9%, with discretionary product categories remaining weak, countered somewhat by continued growth in food, beverage, and beauty segments. Store sales performed better than digital sales, and same-day fulfillment services such as Drive-Up saw robust growth, up 12%. However, customer traffic decreased 4.1% and average transaction value edged down 0.8%. For Q4, Target projects a further mid-single-digit decline in comparable sales. The fiscal fourth quarter includes an additional (53rd) week, projected to add approximately $1.7 billion in sales, though this will not affect comparable sales metrics.
Profitability has been a clear bright spot for Target. Q3 GAAP and adjusted earnings per share reached $2.10, more than 36% higher than the previous year and over 50% above 2019 levels. Operating margin in Q3 reached 5.2%, over one percentage point above last year and only slightly below pre-pandemic Q3 2019 despite the continuing impact of inventory shrinkage. Operating margin dollars exceeded those from 2019 by more than 30%. Gross margin improved to 27.4%, a gain of more than 2.5 percentage points year-over-year, driven by reductions in freight costs, clearance markdowns, and supply chain expenses along with a more profitable category mix. Although SG&A expense rate rose to 20.9%—up just over one percentage point due largely to labor investments, cost inflation, and the negative impact of lower sales—these increases were partially contained through disciplined cost controls. Depreciation and amortization expenses were modestly higher at 2.4% of revenue.
Looking at the year to date, through the first three quarters of 2023, Target’s EPS is over 40% higher than last year and over 26% higher than in 2019. Operating income for this period increased by more than $1 billion year-over-year despite retail shrink and softer sales volumes. Full-year EPS guidance is maintained in the range of $7.75 to $8.75, despite the challenging sales environment. Fourth quarter EPS guidance ranges from $1.90 to $2.60, up to 37% above the prior year, with the extra week expected to add roughly 30 basis points to operating margin. The trailing 12-month after-tax return on invested capital (ROIC) for Q3 was 13.9%, down slightly from 14.6% a year ago but improving sequentially.
Target’s cash flow and capital allocation were strong. Operating cash flow for the first three quarters exceeded $5.3 billion, a major improvement from approximately $550 million during the comparable 2022 period, thanks to better profitability and improved working capital, mainly through tighter inventory management. Capital expenditures were just under $4 billion year-to-date and are expected to end the year toward the upper end of $4–$5 billion. There were no share repurchases in Q3, as the company prioritized liquidity and credit quality. Dividends paid in Q3 slightly increased to just over $0.5 billion, reflecting a 1.9% per-share dividend increase versus last year.
Operationally, Target improved efficiency and inventory management, critical for profit gains. Year-over-year inventory at the end of Q3 was down 14%, with a 19% drop in discretionary inventory. While total inventory is 29% above Q3 2019 levels, this aligns with increased sales volume over the same time. These inventory improvements contributed to better gross margins and lower SG&A expenses. The company also improved its in-stock position, especially for key items and in smaller format stores, supporting both sales and profitability. Supply chain metrics, such as lead times and order fill rates, also improved. Efficiency initiatives delivered hundreds of millions of dollars in cost savings through 2023.
Other financial highlights include a modest profitability lift from a shift toward better-margin categories like Beauty and away from less profitable electronics. Inventory shrink remained a notable headwind, accounting for a 40 basis point drag on gross margin in Q3, but this was less severe than expected.
In summary, despite macroeconomic headwinds and ongoing weakness in discretionary spending, Target delivered a strong year of profitability improvements, driven by operational execution, cost controls, beneficial inventory management, and efficiency gains. Revenue showed short-term declines, but EBIT, margins, gross profit, and EPS all increased both year-over-year and versus pre-pandemic benchmarks. Target expects some continued sales softness in the near term but remains committed to long-term, disciplined investment in stores, digital capabilities, assortment, and its workforce to support ongoing top- and bottom-line growth.

Financial results for Home Depot:
Home Depot reported a decline in revenue and profit for the third quarter compared to the same period last year. Total sales were $37.7 billion, which represents a 3% decrease, or $1.2 billion less than the prior year. Comparable sales decreased 3.1% overall, with U.S. comparable sales down by 3.5%. Gross margin fell slightly to 33.8%, down about 20 basis points. Operating expenses increased to 19.4% of sales, rising by 120 basis points, which drove a reduction in operating margin to 14.3% from last year’s 15.8%. Operating profits were further impacted by higher interest and other expenses totaling $438 million, up by $30 million year-over-year, although the effective tax rate decreased to 23.3% from 24.4%.
Diluted earnings per share (EPS) declined to $3.81, down 10.1% from $4.24. Return on invested capital also decreased to 38.7% from 43.3% in the previous year. Inventory management improved with inventory declining to $22.8 billion, down by $2.9 billion or 11%, while inventory turns remained flat at 4.3 times annually.
Digital and online sales saw a positive trend, increasing about 5% over last year’s third quarter, and nearly half of these online orders were fulfilled through physical stores, reflecting an integrated omni-channel approach.
For capital allocation, during the third quarter, Home Depot invested approximately $670 million in capital expenditures while returning $2.1 billion to shareholders through dividends and $1.5 billion through share repurchases. As of the end of the quarter, Home Depot operated 2,333 stores totaling 242 million square feet of retail selling space.
Looking forward, Home Depot narrowed its guidance for fiscal 2023, projecting overall sales and comparable sales to decline between 3% and 4%, with operating margin expected to be between 14.2% and 14.1%. The projected effective tax rate is approximately 24.5%, and interest expense for the full year is estimated at $1.8 billion. Diluted EPS is now expected to decline between 9% and 11% versus fiscal 2022.
The decline in revenue and profit was attributed to several factors, including shifts in customer behavior favoring smaller projects over large ones and pressure in high-ticket discretionary purchases. Commodity deflation, particularly in lumber and copper, led to lower average ticket sizes, while transactions above $1,000 were down 5.2%. The Pro customer segment performed better than DIY but is experiencing lower backlogs than last year, though they are still historically elevated.
Profitability has also been pressured by higher operating expenses, largely due to planned increases in compensation, as well as the operating deleverage resulting from lower total sales. In response, Home Depot continues to focus on long-term growth through ongoing investments in its digital experience, store productivity, and Pro customer initiatives, and has targeted $500 million in annualized cost savings as part of its 2024 outlook.
Overall, Home Depot’s third quarter results reflect a challenging economic environment with both lower revenues and profits, but the company remains committed to shareholder returns and long-term investments designed to support future revenue and profit growth.

Financial results for Broadcom:
In fiscal Q4 2023, Broadcom reported consolidated net revenue of $9.3 billion, representing a 4% year-on-year increase. Semiconductor Solutions made up $7.3 billion (79% of total revenue, up 3% YoY), while Infrastructure Software contributed $2 billion (21% of total, up 7% YoY). The overall gross margin was 74.3%, operating income reached $5.7 billion (up 4% YoY, 62% of revenue), and adjusted EBITDA was $6 billion (65% of revenue). Free cash flow for the quarter was strong at $4.7 billion, making up 51% of revenue. Operating expenses were flat at $1.2 billion, with R&D steady at $940 million. Within the segments, Semiconductor Solutions saw operating profit growth of 2% YoY and an operating margin of 58%. Infrastructure Software delivered a 12% YoY increase in operating profit with a 75% operating margin.
For the full fiscal year 2023, Broadcom achieved record consolidated revenue of $35.8 billion, up 8% from the prior year. Semiconductor Solutions contributed $28.2 billion (up 9% YoY), and Infrastructure Software $7.6 billion (up 3% YoY). Operating income was $22.1 billion (9% increase YoY, 62% of net revenue), and adjusted EBITDA grew 10% year-on-year to $23.2 billion (65% of net revenue, excluding $502 million in depreciation). Annual free cash flow reached $17.6 billion, up 8% year-on-year and representing 49% of revenue. The gross margin for the year was 74.7% (down 90 basis points YoY), while operating expenses declined 4% to $4.6 billion. Capital expenditures for the year were $452 million.
In 2023, Broadcom returned $15.3 billion to shareholders—$7.6 billion through dividends and $7.7 billion via share repurchases and eliminations. At year-end, the authorized balance remaining for share buyback was $7.2 billion. The company's balance sheet was strong, with $14.2 billion in cash, $39.2 billion in gross debt (including $1.6 billion short term), inventory of $1.9 billion, 31 days sales outstanding, and 76 days inventory on hand.
Looking ahead to fiscal year 2024, Broadcom projected a substantial increase in consolidated revenue to $50 billion (up 40% YoY), reflecting the acquisition of VMware (closed November 22, 2023). Semiconductor Solutions revenue is expected to grow in the mid- to high single digits, while Infrastructure Software revenue is forecast at $20 billion ($8 billion from legacy Broadcom software and $12 billion from VMware). The integration of VMware will involve an estimated $1 billion in transition spending during FY24, with efforts underway to refocus on core private/hybrid cloud businesses and divest non-core ventures such as end-user computing and Carbon Black. Adjusted EBITDA is projected at approximately 60% of total revenue for 2024.
The company announced a significant increase in its quarterly dividend for Q1 FY24, up 14% to $5.25 per share, implying an annual dividend of $21 per share—a record level and the 13th consecutive annual increase.
Reviewing semiconductor segment trends, Networking posted strong growth in FY23, rising 21% YoY to $10.8 billion (Q4: $3.1 billion, up 23% YoY), with FY24 networking revenue expected to grow an additional 30% YoY. Wireless revenue was $7.3 billion (down 2% YoY, expected stable in FY24), server storage connectivity was $4.5 billion (up 11% YoY, expected to decline mid- to high teens in FY24), broadband was $4.5 billion (up 8% YoY, outlook for a low- to mid-teens YoY decline), and industrial revenue was $962 million (expected down low single digits in FY24). AI-related revenue is an increasingly important driver: in FY23, generative AI represented 15% of semiconductor revenue, with Q4 FY23 hitting nearly $1.5 billion (~20% of segment revenue); FY24 guidance expects generative AI to account for over 25% of the semiconductor segment.
Broadcom also demonstrated strong recurring software revenue, with renewal rates at 119-130% in Q4 and over 90% coming from recurring business. The VMware strategy involves shifting perpetual licenses toward a subscription-based model by the end of FY24. Overall, the company delivered record profit and robust revenue growth for the year, underpinned by an emphasis on high-margin, cash-generative businesses, strong shareholder returns, and ambitious revenue growth expectations anchored by the VMware integration and expanding AI opportunity.

Financial results for Ulta Beauty:
In the third quarter, Ulta Beauty reported net sales of $2.5 billion, up 6.4% year-over-year, with comparable sales increasing by 4.5%. Digital channel sales grew by about 10%. Operating profit for the quarter was $327.2 million, representing 13.1% of net sales, down from 15.5% in the third quarter of the previous year. Gross margin declined to 39.9% from 41.2% due to lower merchandise margins, higher inventory shrink, and increased costs in the supply chain. Selling, general and administrative expenses rose 10.8% to $661.4 million, and reflected a higher share of net sales (26.6% versus 25.5%). Diluted earnings per share were $5.07, a decrease of 5.1% from $5.34 last year, and overall operating income dropped 9.6% year-over-year. Store transactions increased 5.9%, suggesting continued healthy traffic, but average ticket size decreased 1.4% as units per transaction fell, even though average selling price rose. Gross margin decreased by 130 basis points compared to last year, which reflected the lapping of price change benefits and more promotional activity.
Year-to-date, Ulta’s inventory reached $2.3 billion, up 9.8% because of new stores, higher demand, inventory to support a new fulfillment center, and higher product costs. Capital expenditures for the quarter totaled $106 million, primarily for stores, supply chain enhancements, and IT. The company repurchased 687,000 shares for $281.5 million in Q3 and has repurchased 1.8 million shares year-to-date at a cost of $840.5 million. Short-term debt stood at $195.4 million, while cash and equivalents were $121.8 million as of quarter end.
For fiscal year 2023, Ulta Beauty raised its net sales guidance to between $11.1 and $11.15 billion, expecting comparable sales growth of 5% to 5.5%. Operating margin guidance remains at 14.6% to 14.8%, with updated diluted EPS guidance now at $25.20 to $25.60. Capital expenditure expectations are between $400 and $425 million for the year, encompassing $180 million for supply chain and IT, $170 million for stores and fixtures, and $60 million for maintenance. Depreciation is anticipated to be about $245 million, and the company plans to repurchase about $950 million in shares. Ulta expects to open 25-30 net new stores and remodel or relocate another 20-30 stores.
During the quarter, profitability was pressured by increased promotional activity (though still below pre-pandemic levels), higher inventory shrink, and higher supply chain costs linked to new fulfillment capacity and related depreciation. Nevertheless, the impact was partially offset by growth in credit card and royalty income, especially from Ulta’s partnership with Target.
From a category standpoint, skincare saw the fastest growth with double-digit gains, while fragrance and bath achieved low-double-digit growth. Makeup sales were flat overall, as gains in mass makeup were offset by a slight decline in prestige makeup. Hair care comp sales declined in the low-single digits, primarily due to weakness in hair tools.
Looking to the fourth quarter and beyond, Ulta expects comparable sales to be flat to modestly up, with gross margins facing modest deleverage and operating margins anticipated to be flat or slightly down. While category fundamentals remain sound, Ulta acknowledges a risk environment that includes more competitive promotions, a greater number of beauty retail locations, and the challenge of lapping strong prior-year performance. Ulta affirms its long-term goals of 3-5% comp sales growth and 14-15% operating margins, and expects earnings per share to grow next year, despite fiscal 2023 including an extra week.
Overall, Ulta Beauty’s quarterly results reflect strong revenue and transaction growth but with some contraction in margins due to increased costs and promotional pressures. The company continues to invest in growth and operational transformation, maintains robust cash return to shareholders through share repurchases, and expects to drive profitable growth, expand its market presence, and keep focusing on operational efficiencies in the future.

Financial results for Intuit
Intuit reported a strong first quarter for fiscal year 2024, with substantial increases in both revenue and profits. Total company revenue grew to $3.0 billion, representing a 15% year-over-year increase. GAAP operating income soared to $307 million, up significantly from $76 million the prior year, while non-GAAP operating income rose to $960 million from $662 million, a 45% increase. GAAP diluted earnings per share were $0.85, well above the previous year's $0.14, and non-GAAP EPS reached $2.47, an impressive 49% jump from $1.66 last year.
Segment performance highlighted the Small Business and Self-Employed Group, where revenue increased by 18%, and the online ecosystem saw a 20% rise. QuickBooks Online Accounting revenue grew 19%, attributed to higher customer counts, improved pricing, and a favorable product mix. Online services revenue, which includes payroll, Mailchimp, payments, capital, and time tracking, also grew 20%. Online payment volume was up 21% for the quarter. In the Desktop Ecosystem business, revenue rose by 14%, with QuickBooks Desktop Enterprise achieving high single-digit growth as Intuit continues transitioning desktop users to a recurring subscription model and implements higher prices across several products.
Credit Karma reported quarterly revenue of $405 million, down 5% from the prior year. This decline was due to macroeconomic pressures, particularly partners' increased caution in extending personal loans and credit cards. Though some growth occurred in credit card and Credit Karma Money products, it was outpaced by declines in personal loans, auto insurance, home and auto loans, resulting in a net decrease in revenue for the segment.
Other notable results came from the Consumer Group, which saw revenue climb to $187 million—a 25% annual increase—reflecting strong performance during the tax extension season and higher TurboTax return volumes. The ProTax group posted $42 million in revenue, up 24% from a year ago. Internationally, total online ecosystem revenue on a constant currency basis grew by 16%.
Intuit's financial position remains solid, ending the quarter with $2.3 billion in cash and investments and $5.9 billion in debt. The company repurchased $603 million in stock during the quarter and raised the quarterly dividend by 15% to $0.90 per share. Intuit also refinanced its debt, issuing $4 billion in senior notes at a 5.29% weighted average coupon, using the proceeds to repay higher-cost term loans.
Looking ahead, Intuit reaffirmed its guidance for fiscal 2024, anticipating total company revenue growth of 11% to 12%, with GAAP operating income growing 15% to 18% and non-GAAP operating income rising 12% to 14%. The company expects GAAP EPS growth of 11% to 15%, and non-GAAP EPS to rise 12% to 14%. For the second quarter, revenue is expected to grow by 11% to 12%, with GAAP EPS between $0.62 and $0.68, and non-GAAP EPS between $2.25 and $2.31.
Overall, Intuit’s Q1 performance exceeded expectations, with robust revenue and profit growth across most segments, especially in Small Business and Consumer. Despite ongoing challenges within Credit Karma due to macroeconomic conditions, Intuit is optimistic about maintaining strong double-digit top- and bottom-line growth for the rest of the fiscal year.

Financial results for GAP Inc.
In Q3 FY2023, GAP Inc. reported net sales (revenue) of $3.8 billion, which was a 7% decrease compared to last year. Comparable sales fell 2%, but this performance still exceeded internal expectations. The company noted that last year’s sale of Gap China negatively impacted sales growth by approximately $70 million or two percentage points. Despite the revenue decline, GAP Inc. significantly improved profitability. Gross margin for the quarter was 41.3%, up 390 basis points over the prior year’s reported rate and 260 basis points above last year’s adjusted rate. This margin improvement was attributed to a 340 basis point increase in merchandise margin, driven by better sourcing (including commodity cost savings and reduced air freight) and lower promotional activity as a result of disciplined inventory management.
The company’s adjusted operating margin reached 6.8%, a rise of 290 basis points from last year. Reported operating income was $250 million, while adjusted operating income excluding restructuring charges was $255 million, an increase of $99 million year over year. SG&A expenses were $1.3 billion; on an adjusted basis, these costs fell by 7% versus the prior year, demonstrating GAP Inc.’s successful expense controls. Earnings per share (EPS) came in at $0.58, with adjusted EPS (excluding restructuring) at $0.59.
Looking at brand performance, Old Navy delivered net sales of $2.13 billion, down just 1% year over year, with comparable sales up 1% and continued market share gains for the third consecutive quarter. The Gap brand saw net sales of $887 million, a decline of 15% from last year, but adjusting for the China divestiture and Yeezy shutdown, net sales were down 6% and comparable sales fell just 1%. Banana Republic experienced a net sales drop of 11% to $460 million, and comparable sales were down 8%. Athleta reported the steepest declines with net sales down 18% to $279 million and comparable sales down 19%, reflecting ongoing challenges from last year’s discounting and repositioning.
Inventory was tightly managed, down 22% year over year and nearly $800 million lower than last year’s peak. This inventory discipline supported margin improvement and contributed to year-to-date free cash flow of $544 million. The balance sheet was notably stronger, with $1.4 billion in cash and equivalents at quarter end—up 99% from the previous year—and the company’s asset-based line of credit was fully repaid and undrawn. Capital expenditures for 2023 were revised down to about $475 million, below initial expectations due to fewer store openings.
Guidance for the full year maintained expectations for net sales to decline by a mid-single-digit percentage compared to FY2022’s $15.6 billion. Fourth-quarter net sales are expected to be flat to slightly negative, but will benefit from the 53rd week, adding approximately $150 million in revenue. Gross margin is expected to expand for both Q4 and the full year, aided by factors such as lower air freight, improved commodity costs, and more effective promotional strategies. Year-end inventory is projected to be about 15% lower than the prior year.
From a shareholder perspective, the company upheld its $0.15 per share quarterly dividend and emphasized its commitment to shareholder returns. Free cash flow and cash position are significantly stronger, and the company’s repayment of its credit line underscores improved financial flexibility.
In summary, despite a decline in overall revenue, GAP Inc. delivered a strong improvement in both gross and operating margins through strategic inventory management, cost control, and reduced promotional activity. Brands Old Navy and Gap are showing positive signs, though Athleta and Banana Republic continue to face headwinds. The company’s improved cash generation, disciplined expense management, and better balance sheet place it in a more favorable position for sustained profitability and future growth, even as it maintains a cautious revenue outlook in an uncertain consumer environment.

Financial results for Apple
Apple reported outstanding financial performance for the September quarter and the full fiscal year, marked by record revenue and profit levels. For the September quarter, total revenue reached $102.5 billion, an 8% increase year-over-year and a new record for the period. Services revenue set an all-time high at $28.8 billion, up 15% year-over-year, while product revenue rose 5% to $73.7 billion, also a September quarter record. The company achieved a gross margin of 47.2%, improving by 70 basis points from the previous quarter, despite absorbing approximately $1.1 billion in tariff-related costs. Product gross margin climbed to 36.2%, and services gross margin was 75.3%. Net income for the quarter hit $27.5 billion, the highest for a September quarter, and diluted earnings per share increased 13% year-over-year to $1.85. Operating cash flow also reached a record $29.7 billion in the quarter.
Breaking down by segments, iPhone revenue grew 6% year-over-year to $49 billion, setting another September quarter record. Mac revenue increased 13% to $8.7 billion, with growth in all geographical regions. iPad revenue was $7 billion, remaining flat year-over-year, as did wearables, home, and accessories at $9 billion. Apple achieved September quarter revenue records in key regions including the U.S., Canada, Latin America, Western Europe, the Middle East, Japan, Korea, South Asia, and recorded all-time highs in India and other emerging markets. Services revenue rose across all geographies, with categories such as advertising, App Store, cloud, music, payments, and video all hitting all-time records. For the full fiscal year, services revenue surpassed $100 billion, a 14% year-over-year increase.
Apple’s full fiscal year results were similarly robust, with total revenue reaching an all-time record of $416 billion, driven by continued growth across iPhone, Mac, iPad, and Services segments. Net income and diluted EPS also set all-time records, with EPS showing double-digit year-over-year growth on an adjusted basis.
Regarding capital, Apple ended the quarter with $132 billion in cash and marketable securities against $99 billion in total debt, resulting in a net cash position of $34 billion. The company returned $24 billion to shareholders during the quarter, including $3.9 billion in dividends and equivalents and $20 billion in share repurchases.
Looking ahead to the December quarter, Apple anticipates revenue growth of 10% to 12% year-over-year, projecting it to be the best quarter ever for both the company and iPhone revenues. Gross margin is expected between 47% and 48%, factoring in an estimated $1.4 billion in tariff costs, and operating expenses are forecast to range from $18.1 billion to $18.5 billion. The effective tax rate is expected to be around 17%. The board declared a cash dividend of $0.26 per share, payable on November 13, 2025.
In summary, Apple’s recent quarterly and annual results showcase record-setting revenue and profit growth across all major product and service categories. The company continues to generate strong cash flow and is maintaining substantial shareholder returns through dividends and stock buybacks. Apple’s guidance indicates confidence in continued momentum and another record-breaking quarter ahead, especially for iPhone sales.

Financial results for Microsoft
Microsoft reported record financial results, with total revenue for the fiscal year exceeding $281 billion, marking a 15% year-over-year increase. Operating income also reached new highs at over $128 billion, up 17% from the prior year, demonstrating sustained profitability alongside revenue growth. The Microsoft Cloud segment was a major contributor, generating more than $168 billion in annual revenue (up 23%), while Azure alone delivered $75 billion, a significant 34% increase from the previous year.
For the fourth quarter, Microsoft posted $76.4 billion in revenue, representing an 18% increase in constant currency. Gross margin dollars rose 15%, and operating income grew by 22% in constant currency, supporting a quarterly EPS of $3.65, up 22%. Microsoft Cloud quarterly revenue reached $46.7 billion (up 25%), and commercial bookings surpassed $100 billion, growing 30%. The commercial remaining performance obligation now stands at $368 billion, up 35%. Free cash flow for the quarter was robust at $25.6 billion.
Breaking it down by segments: Productivity and Business Processes achieved $33.1 billion in revenue (up 14% in constant currency), powered by Microsoft 365 commercial cloud (revenue up 16%), Microsoft 365 consumer cloud (up 20% revenue and 8% subscriber growth following a price increase), and Dynamics 365 (up 21%). LinkedIn revenue also rose by 8%. Segment gross margin dollars increased 15% and operating income by 19%. The Intelligent Cloud segment delivered $29.9 billion in revenue (25% growth in constant currency), with Azure and other cloud services collectively rising by 39%. Conversely, the on-premises server business saw a 3% decline. Intelligent Cloud operating income was up 23%. The More Personal Computing segment grew revenue by 9% to $13.5 billion, with Windows OEM and devices revenue up 3%, search and news advertising growing 20%, and gaming revenue advancing by 10%, including a 12% increase in Xbox content and services. Operating income for this segment surged by 33%.
Margins remain a focal point: company gross margin percentage was 69% (down 1 point year-over-year), affected by the shift toward Azure and sustained AI/datacenter investment. Microsoft Cloud’s gross margin narrowed slightly to 68%. Nevertheless, corporate operating margin improved to 45%, up 2 percentage points year-over-year, reflecting strong operating leverage amid higher revenue. Operating expenses increased moderately by 5%, with segment-wise growth of 6% in Productivity and Business Processes, 4% in Intelligent Cloud, and 3% in More Personal Computing. Year-end headcount was stable.
Capital expenditures in Q4 totaled $24.2 billion (including $5 billion in finance leases), with $17.1 billion spent on property, plant, and equipment. Cash flow from operations was $42.6 billion, up 15%, and free cash flow matched the previously noted $25.6 billion. Microsoft returned $9.4 billion to shareholders via dividends and share repurchases during the quarter ($37+ billion for the full year). The effective tax rate for the fiscal year was approximately 17%.
Looking ahead, Microsoft anticipates another year of double-digit revenue and operating income growth in fiscal year 2026, with an expected effective tax rate of 19–20%. Capital expenditures for the upcoming quarter are projected to exceed $30 billion. Segments are forecast to continue strong growth: Productivity and Business Processes revenue of $32.2–$32.5 billion (14–15% growth), Intelligent Cloud revenue of $30.1–$30.4 billion (25–26% growth) with Azure’s growth expected around 37%, and More Personal Computing at $12.4–$12.9 billion. Company-wide, cost of goods sold is expected at $24.3–$24.5 billion (up 21–22%), and operating expenses at $15.7–$15.8 billion.
Overall, the key drivers of Microsoft’s performance include the exceptional growth of the Azure and broader Microsoft Cloud businesses, as well as solid expansion in Dynamics 365 and gaming (including Xbox and Game Pass). Cloud-related revenue remains the primary growth and profit engine, setting the foundation for continued shareholder returns and heavy but disciplined investment in AI and datacenter infrastructure. While margins have softened slightly due to ongoing infrastructure investments, the strength of top-line growth and operating leverage have contributed to expanding operating margins and rising free cash flow.

Financial results for Alphabet (Google):
Alphabet (Google) achieved strong financial performance in 2025, reporting consolidated full-year revenues of $403 billion, an increase of 15% year-over-year. Net income rose 30% to $34.5 billion, with earnings per share up 31% to $2.82. The company generated substantial cash, with operating cash flow for the year at $160.5 billion and free cash flow totaling $73.3 billion.
For Q4 2025, revenue reached $113.8 billion, reflecting 17% year-over-year growth driven by acceleration in Search and Cloud. Operating income for the quarter was $35.9 billion (up 16%), marking a 31.6% operating margin. Net income for Q4 was $34.5 billion, up 30% compared to last year. Operating cash flow in Q4 reached a record $52.4 billion, while free cash flow was $24.6 billion. By the end of Q4, Alphabet held $120.8 billion in cash and marketable securities, with long-term debt of $46.5 billion.
Within the Google Services segment, which includes Search, YouTube, Ads, Subscriptions, and Devices, revenues for Q4 were $95.9 billion (14% YoY growth) and operating income was $40.1 billion (22% YoY growth), reflecting a robust 41.9% operating margin. Search and other advertising revenues were $63.1 billion, up 17%, demonstrating strong performance across major verticals, especially retail. YouTube advertising revenues reached $11.4 billion (up 9%), supported by direct response ad growth, though U.S. election spend from the prior year created difficult comparison. Network advertising revenues were $7.8 billion, declining 2%. Subscriptions, platforms, and devices revenues grew 17% to $13.6 billion, led by rapid growth in YouTube subscriptions and Google One, with increased demand for AI-powered plans.
The Google Cloud segment posted Q4 revenues of $17.7 billion, achieving 48% annual growth and an accelerating pace, largely attributed to strong enterprise adoption of AI products and services. Cloud operating income more than doubled YoY to $5.3 billion, with the margin rising to 30.1% (up from 17.5% the prior year). Cloud backlog grew rapidly to $240 billion, rising 55% sequentially and more than doubling year-over-year, reflecting demand for AI infrastructure, enterprise cloud, security, and data analytics. GCP secured a significantly higher number of billion-dollar-plus deals compared to the prior three years combined, driven by new product offerings like Gemini and broader customer adoption.
Other Bets, including Waymo, delivered $370 million in Q4 revenue but posted an operating loss of $3.6 billion in the quarter, which included a $2.1 billion charge related to Waymo’s higher valuation following a major investment round. Alphabet actively participated in Waymo’s $16 billion fundraise to support its global expansion.
On the expense side, Alphabet’s cost of revenue increased 13% in Q4 to $45.8 billion. R&D costs surged 42%, primarily for increased AI and Waymo investments. Sales and marketing expenses climbed 12%, and general/administrative costs were up 21%. Depreciation grew sharply in 2025, up 38% to $21.1 billion due to elevated capital investments in infrastructure. Full-year capital expenditures were $91.4 billion (with $27.9 billion in Q4), mostly (60%) channelled into servers and the remainder into data centers and networking.
Alphabet continued to return capital to shareholders, with $5.5 billion in share repurchases and $2.5 billion in dividends during Q4 2025.
Looking ahead to 2026, Alphabet anticipates capital expenditures in the range of $175–$185 billion, reflecting continued, aggressive investment in AI compute capacity and data center buildout to capture accelerating growth in cloud and AI-driven services. Backlog and cloud demand remain robust, with generative AI fueling top-line expansion but also contributing to continued expense pressure, especially depreciation and data center costs.
Key business achievements for the year include YouTube surpassing $60 billion in annual revenue (from ads and subscriptions), rapid growth in YouTube Premium and Music subscriptions, Cloud exceeding a $70 billion annual run rate, and more than 325 million paid subscriptions across Alphabet’s consumer offerings. Enterprise adoption of Gemini AI and other Cloud AI solutions has been particularly strong, with over 8 million paid Gemini Enterprise seats sold in just four months post-launch.
In summary, Alphabet (Google) delivered record revenue and profit growth in 2025, driven by the strength of its core advertising and cloud businesses, the rapid adoption of AI products, and disciplined investment in infrastructure for future growth. Operating and net margins remain healthy, and the company continues to generate strong cash flows supporting ongoing investment and shareholder returns.

Financial results for Amazon.com, Inc.
Amazon reported strong financial results for the period, highlighted by significant year-over-year growth in revenue and operating income. Worldwide revenue reached $213.4 billion, marking a 12% increase when excluding foreign exchange effects. The North America segment contributed $127.1 billion in revenue, a 10% rise from the prior year, while the International segment delivered $50.7 billion, up 11% year over year excluding currency impacts. Amazon Web Services (AWS) generated $35.6 billion in revenue for the quarter, representing a 24% year-over-year growth rate and a quarter-over-quarter increase of $2.6 billion, positioning AWS with an annualized revenue run rate of $142 billion. The advertising segment also saw strong results, with $21.3 billion in revenue and a 22% annual growth rate, contributing over $12 billion of new revenue in 2025 alone. Gross sales in the grocery business exceeded $150 billion, while Amazon’s chips division, including Graviton and Trainium, surpassed a $10 billion annualized revenue run rate, with both experiencing rapid, triple-digit and double-digit growth, respectively.
On the profitability front, Amazon reported worldwide operating income of $25 billion for the quarter, though this was impacted by $2.4 billion in special charges, including costs associated with legal matters, severance, and asset impairments. The North America segment delivered $11.5 billion in operating income with a 9% margin, up from 8% the previous year, while the International segment had $1 billion in operating income and a 2.1% margin, reflecting improvement when excluding special charges. AWS operating income was $12.5 billion, supported by ongoing infrastructure investments and strong demand for cloud and AI services. On a trailing twelve-month basis, free cash flow totaled $11.2 billion, and full-year operating cash flow climbed 20% to $139.5 billion due to higher operating income and working capital improvements.
Special charges totaling $2.4 billion had a direct impact on profitability, arising from a $1.1 billion charge related to tax matters and litigation (primarily affecting the International segment), $730 million in estimated severance across all business units, and $610 million in asset impairments mainly tied to physical stores in North America.
Key business drivers included a 61% mix of third-party seller units in Q4 and a 12% increase in paid units worldwide, the highest quarterly growth rate for Amazon in 2025. AWS continued to expand its global data center capacity at an industry-leading pace. Operational efficiencies helped Amazon achieve its fastest-ever Prime delivery speeds, with US Prime members receiving over 8 billion items via same- or next-day delivery—over 30% higher than the prior year. Investment activity remains high, with projected capital expenditures of around $200 billion, mainly allocated to AWS. The Amazon LEO satellite initiative is also progressing, with many launch expenses currently recognized as incurred, but expected to be capitalized later in the year.
Looking ahead, Amazon provided guidance for the next quarter, forecasting net sales between $173.5 billion and $178.5 billion, with a positive currency effect of about 180 basis points. Expected operating income for Q1 is in the range of $16.5 billion to $21.5 billion.
By segment, the North America region reported improved operating margins at 9%, up from 8% a year earlier. International margins stood at 2.1%. AWS remained a key profit driver, earning $12.5 billion in operating income during the quarter. The company’s profitability figures reflect the continuing strength of core businesses, alongside steady improvements in cash generation and operational efficiency.
In summary, Amazon.com's latest results demonstrate robust revenue and profit growth across major business lines, despite incurring $2.4 billion in special charges that temporarily impacted operating income. AWS, Advertising, Grocery, and the chip division showed especially strong growth and momentum. Investments remain elevated in infrastructure and new initiatives, potentially influencing future margins, but current metrics reflect substantial year-over-year improvement in profitability, cash flows, and operational scale.

Financial results for Tesla, Inc.
Tesla ended 2025 with strong performance in its energy segment, achieving approximately $12.8 billion in energy revenue, marking a 26.6% year-over-year increase driven by high demand for MegaPack and Powerwall products across all regions. The company reported a larger backlog than in previous years, especially in international markets such as Malaysia, Norway, Poland, Saudi Arabia, and Taiwan. Tesla saw record deployments in storage and expects further revenue increases as new products, like MegaPack 3 and Mega Block, are introduced in 2026.
In terms of profitability and margins, the automotive gross margin, excluding regulatory credits, improved sequentially from 15.4% to 17.9%, despite a 16% decline in deliveries, which was offset by a favorable sales mix weighted towards APAC and EMEA regions. Automotive gross profit remained flat quarter-over-quarter. The energy segment posted another record for gross profit in the quarter. Total gross margin for the quarter exceeded 20.1% for the first time in over two years, showing improvement even with lower fixed cost absorption and the negative impact of tariffs.
The “Services and Other” segment experienced a margin decline from 10.5% to 8.8%, mostly due to higher employee expenses linked to the expansion of service centers, though supercharging contributed some margin improvement. Full Self-Driving (FSD) adoption rose to nearly 1.1 million paid users globally, with around 70% of these customers opting for upfront purchases. However, Tesla has shifted to a subscription-only model for FSD, which is expected to create some short-term pressure on automotive margins.
Operating expenses increased by $500 million in Q4, mainly due to higher stock-based compensation, costs related to the Operation Maestro performance award, and sustained heavy investment in AI, new products (such as CyberCab, Semi, Optimus, and MegaPack), as well as increased spending on R&D and capital expenditures. Net income was adversely affected by mark-to-market losses on Bitcoin holdings (which fell 23% quarter-over-quarter) and foreign exchange losses originating from large intercompany borrowings.
Tesla finished the year with $1.4 billion in free cash flow. Capital expenditures for 2025 were just under $9 billion, slightly below prior guidance. Looking ahead to 2026, CapEx is projected to surpass $20 billion, reflecting significant investments in six factories (including those for refinery, LFP production, CyberCab, Semi, Mega, and Optimus), AI compute infrastructure, expansion of existing facilities, robotaxi operations, and further development of the Optimus robotics platform. Additional future CapEx might include investments in solar cell manufacturing and “tariff fab” initiatives.
Tesla anticipates margin compression in the energy segment going forward due to increased low-cost competition, regulatory uncertainties, and tariffs. Nonetheless, the company believes that its substantial CapEx plans are essential to strategically position Tesla for long-term growth, especially in the areas of autonomy, robotics, and energy. Management remains focused on capital efficiency even amid elevated investment levels, with these expenditures expected to fuel the next phase of Tesla’s evolution. The year 2026 is highlighted as pivotal for new ventures and product launches.
In summary, Tesla closed 2025 with robust growth in energy revenue, notable improvement in key margins despite lower automotive deliveries and increased costs, and maintained strong free cash flow while gearing up for extensive investment heading into 2026. While profitability was temporarily impacted by external factors like cryptocurrency revaluation and currency losses, the company’s aggressive CapEx and ongoing investments in autonomy, AI, and robotics signal continued commitment to expanding and diversifying its revenue and profit streams in the years ahead.

Financial results for Meta
Meta delivered strong financial results for Q4 2025, demonstrating robust revenue and profit growth, particularly in its Family of Apps segment. Total Family of Apps revenue reached $58.9 billion, marking a 25% year-over-year increase, while ad revenue, which makes up the majority of the total, grew by 24% to $58.1 billion. Growth in ad revenue was driven by an 18% increase in ad impressions and a 6% rise in average ad prices, signaling heightened advertiser demand and improved ad performance. Other revenue within the Family of Apps, primarily from WhatsApp paid features and MetaVerified subscriptions, jumped 54% to $801 million. Notably, WhatsApp paid messaging exceeded a $2 billion annual run rate in the quarter. Meanwhile, Reality Labs, Meta's segment focused on AR/VR and related technologies, saw revenue decline by 12% to $955 million, largely due to a tough comparison with the previous year’s product launches and retail activity.
From a cash and profitability standpoint, Meta generated $14.1 billion in free cash flow in Q4 2025 and ended the year with $81.6 billion in cash and marketable securities, alongside $58.7 billion in debt. The company plans to continue funding aggressive infrastructure investment in 2026, especially in AI, through strong internal cash generation and, where needed, cost-effective external financing.
Looking forward, Meta expects full-year 2026 total expenses to range from $162 billion to $169 billion, with the main increases coming from infrastructure investments and compensation for technical, especially AI-focused, talent. Capital expenditures are projected to rise sharply to between $115 billion and $135 billion as Meta accelerates AI and Superintelligence Lab investments. Despite this, operating income for 2026 is forecast to exceed 2025’s level, reflecting strong underlying business performance. The 2026 effective tax rate is projected between 13% and 16%.
Meta’s revenue gains have been fueled by higher user engagement and better monetization. Facebook and Instagram recorded robust growth in content views, Threads achieved a 20% increase in user time spent, and Meta enjoyed the strongest quarterly revenue benefit from Facebook product launches in two years. The expanding rollout of ads on Threads, WhatsApp, and click-to-message formats, particularly in new regions, contributed to these gains. Click-to-message ad revenue in the US rose more than 50% year-over-year, and paid messaging adoption is growing globally. Additionally, investment in AI has supported a 3% lift in conversion rates and a 12% improvement in ad quality in Q4 2025, further bolstering advertising revenue.
Reality Labs continues to operate at a loss, which is expected to persist at the 2025 level throughout 2026. However, management anticipates 2025 as the peak loss year for this segment, with a gradual decrease ahead as VR and wearable investments shift toward profitability.
For Q1 2026, Meta projects total revenue between $53.5 billion and $56.5 billion, with a 4% benefit from foreign exchange. The cost base will rise primarily due to ongoing infrastructure projects and continued AI hiring. Ongoing strong cash flow is expected to sustain these investments, and operating income in 2026 should surpass that of 2025 despite these substantial investments.
Meta also notes persistent legal and regulatory risks, particularly in the US and EU concerning privacy, ad regulation, and youth protection, which could adversely impact future results.
In summary, Meta’s Q4 2025 and outlook for 2026 are characterized by strong top-line and profitability momentum in its core digital advertising business, significant investment in AI and infrastructure, and ongoing discipline in capital allocation, with continued headwinds expected from regulatory challenges.

Financial results for NVIDIA
NVIDIA delivered record-breaking financial performance in the third quarter of fiscal 2025, with both revenue and profit reaching historic highs.
