Total revenue for the quarter was $35.1 billion, reflecting a 17% sequential increase from the previous quarter and an impressive 94% year-over-year growth, notably surpassing the company’s projected outlook of $32.5 billion. Data center revenue was the primary growth engine, accounting for $30.8 billion, up 17% quarter-over-quarter and 112% compared to the same period last year. This surge is attributed to expanding adoption of NVIDIA accelerated computing and AI infrastructure across cloud service providers and enterprises. Networking revenue also demonstrated robust performance, rising 20% year-over-year, with particular strength in segments such as InfiniBand, Ethernet switches, SmartNICs, BlueField DPUs, and a threefold increase in Spectrum-X Ethernet for AI revenues.
Other business segments also contributed meaningfully. Gaming revenue totaled $3.3 billion, up 14% sequentially and 15% year-over-year due to continued demand for RTX GPUs and strong results across notebooks, desktops, and consoles. Professional Visualization reached $486 million, growing 7% sequentially and 17% over the previous year, while automotive revenue hit $449 million, marking a 30% rise sequentially and 72% year-over-year. Recurring software, service, and support revenue is annualizing at $1.5 billion, with expectations to exceed an annual run rate of $2 billion by year-end. Furthermore, NVIDIA AI Enterprise full-year revenue is forecasted to more than double compared to last year, reflecting the increasing significance of software-driven solutions in the company’s revenue mix.
Profitability metrics remained strong despite a minor sequential decline in margins, attributed to a product mix shift within the data center segment toward more complex, higher-cost H100 systems. The GAAP gross margin stood at 74.6%, while the non-GAAP gross margin was 75%. Operating expenses grew 9% sequentially, largely from higher investment in compute, infrastructure, and engineering development. NVIDIA also returned $11.2 billion to shareholders during the quarter via share repurchases and cash dividends.
Looking ahead to the fourth quarter of FY2025, NVIDIA forecasts continued expansion with expected revenue of $37.5 billion, plus or minus 2%, underpinned by sustained excess demand—especially for the new Blackwell AI products. Blackwell-related revenue in Q4 is predicted to surpass prior projections (“several billion dollars”), further boosting the company’s outlook. GAAP gross margin is anticipated to come in at 73% (plus or minus 50 basis points), with non-GAAP margin at 73.5%. Operating expenses are expected to reach $4.8 billion on a GAAP basis or $3.4 billion on a non-GAAP basis, and tax rates are projected to average around 16.5%.
Operationally, NVIDIA sees major momentum in its data center business as hyperscale and enterprise customers accelerate AI infrastructure buildout. About half of NVIDIA’s data center sales come from cloud service providers, with this revenue doubling year-over-year. The company is also seeing strong international customer traction, particularly in India and Japan, where significant investments in NVIDIA GPUs and AI solutions are underway. Gaming remains healthy due to persistent consumer demand, positive channel inventory trends, and strong sales across platforms. Software and AI Enterprise revenue streams are growing, with monetization rates doubling compared to the prior year, highlighting the company’s successful pivot toward higher margin, recurring revenue models.
Looking further, NVIDIA expects gross margins to edge down into the low 70s as production of Blackwell AI systems ramps up, but to stabilize in the mid-70s range at scale. Overall, the third quarter of FY2025 illustrates NVIDIA’s leadership in accelerated computing and AI, robust revenue, and substantial profit growth, with a strong outlook for further expansion in the final quarter of the fiscal year, fueled by new product launches and ongoing AI adoption across industries.
